key_findings: INCUMBENT (Untappd) is stagnating and alienating both sides of its network. Owned by Next Glass, Untappd runs the classic check-in + badge gamification loop and monetizes via (a) consumer 'Untappd Insiders' subscription ($5.99/mo or $54.99/yr, which paywalls granular rating scales like .25/.10 and Super-Style badges) and (b) the far larger 'Untappd for Business' SaaS for venues. It is 'Poor'-rated 2.3/5 on Trustpilot. Consumer gripes: intrusive/frequent ads, freezing/hanging bugs, aggressive email/junk-list re-subscription even after account deletion, and resentment that rating precision is paywalled (seen as compromising the integrity of the community score). In Oct/Nov 2025 Untappd expanded beyond beer into wine, spirits, sake, RTDs, THC drinks, coffee, soda and non-alcoholic — a defensive land-grab that its beer-purist base views as mission drift.

BUSINESS-SIDE REVOLT is the loudest, most quantified weakness. Untappd for Business pricing roughly DOUBLED in five years: ~$599/yr to $899 to a two-tier split topping out at $1,199/yr, with NO grandfathering and previously-included features migrated behind a Premium paywall. Venues also complain menus 'look like Untappd' (weak white-labeling) and digital menu displays fail on Fire TV during peak service (Untappd maintains a dedicated troubleshooting article for it). Breweries are actively defecting to BeerMenus, Evergreen (TapHunter), Brewlytics and DigitalPour — and many fear low ratings tanking sales.

STRUCTURAL RATING DISTRUST is Untappd's deepest, least-fixable flaw. Good Beer Hunting's 'Tyranny of the Tickers' documents that Untappd raters are <1% of US beer drinkers, skewed toward intensely-flavored high-ABV styles: Double IPAs average ~4.7 stars while Kolsch caps ~4.1. Users routinely 1-2 star a beer because they dislike the STYLE, not the beer (spawning @untappdwtf). This 'ticker' culture distorts brewery behavior (abandoning lagers/traditional styles to chase scores) and homogenizes tap lists toward hazy IPAs and pastry stouts. Gatekeeping cascades downstream: Tavour requires 5 beers rated >4.0 with 100+ ratings, a bar even Saison Dupont fails. The community does not trust the number.

THE RATING WARS SHOOK OUT: RateBeer (AB InBev/ZX Ventures-owned) fully SHUT DOWN Feb 1, 2025 after craft drinkers boycotted Big-Beer ownership and AB InBev neglected it; ratings export closed Jan 2025. BeerAdvocate (founded 1996; forums + magazine + events) was acquired by Next Glass (Untappd's parent) — consolidating legacy beer-review data under one roof but leaving BA's dated forum UX largely as-is. Net effect: the encyclopedic/forum niche is depleted and consolidated, and one of two historic rating brands is gone, freeing users and their loyalty.

UX GOLD STANDARD is Vivino (wine), and it is a commerce machine, not a check-in game. Vivino = point camera at label -> instant crowd rating, tasting notes, price. 70M+ downloads, 2.7B label scans, 280M+ ratings, 17M wines, ~100k ratings/DAY. It processes reviews into signal ('75% of reviews mentioned oak'). Crucially it monetizes COMMERCE not subscriptions: ~15% commission on wine sales, ~$489M e-commerce net sales (2023), plus Premium (~$4/mo), a Wine Club, advertising to wineries, and data sales. Weakness to exploit: it aggregates all vintages into one score (blurring quality) and recently moved wine-LIST scanning behind a paywall, annoying users. The scan-first, zero-friction, buy-in-app model is the template a beer app should emulate.

SPIRITS REFERENCE (Distiller) shows the taste-discovery model beer lacks. Distiller uses crowd 'Flavor Tags' to auto-generate a per-spirit Flavor Profile and offers 'Flavor Search' (discover by taste), barcode scan, and TruePrice historical/collection valuation. ~60k spirits, millions of reviews. Distiller Pro is $4.99/mo or $48/yr and paywalls the best discovery tools (flavor search, barcode scan, export). Takeaway: taste-graph discovery + collection valuation resonates, but locking discovery behind a paywall is the recurring industry mistake a FREE app can weaponize.

THE LONG TAIL is fragmented and each app owns only one job. Pintley = taste-based beer recommendations (free, but low momentum/dated). BeerMenus = venue tap-list discovery + alerts, claims 7.3M users, focused purely on 'what's on tap near me' (explicitly not a rating/check-in app). Tavour = daily-drop rare craft-beer e-commerce/delivery (600+ breweries, flat $16.90 crate shipping, no subscription required) — beloved product but plagued by shipping/3rd-party-courier failures, sold-out-on-tap notifications, and refund/CS complaints. No single app unifies discovery + taste-match + where-to-buy/drink + purchase.

2024-2026 UPSTARTS validate the emerging wedge. Consumer side: Drinkist explicitly positions against Untappd's beer-first bias and fragmentation — one AI-label-scan tasting journal across beer/wine/spirits/coffee/tea, 'one searchable archive,' pitched to Untappd migrants wanting to keep beer history; adjacent names include Pint Please, Brewver, Taphouse (data-portability angle). Business side: Brewlytics reads POS data to build a taste profile per customer and fires taste-segmented 'Release Radar' alerts. The market is independently converging on: AI scan-first UX + unified multi-beverage log + taste-graph personalization + data ownership.

MACRO TAILWIND favors a broader, less-alcohol-centric app. No/low-alcohol volumes forecast ~4%/yr growth 2024-2028 (no-alcohol +7%); US adult NA beverages to exceed $1B off-premise in 2025; ~49% of Americans trying to drink less in 2025 (+44% since 2023); 27% of 21-29s drink NA weekly; social media is the #1 discovery channel for new NA beverages. An app that treats NA beer, hop water, RTDs and 'mindful drinking' as first-class — not bolted on defensively like Untappd's 2025 expansion — rides the demographic wave the incumbent is fighting.
recommendations: EXPLOIT THE SINGLE CLEAREST WEDGE: a FREE, global, scan-first 'Vivino for beer' that unifies the whole journey. Untappd owns check-in but is beer-biased, ad-cluttered, paywalls precision, and is distrusted; Vivino owns scan-and-commerce but only for wine; the long-tail apps each do one job. The wedge is a single free app that combines Vivino's frictionless AI label/barcode scan-and-rate UX + a trustworthy taste-match score + 'where to drink/buy it near me' + in-app purchase/delivery — with NO paywall on core rating, discovery, or precision. Every incumbent monetizes by locking these away; winning means giving them away and monetizing commerce instead.

MONETIZE COMMERCE, NOT THE COMMUNITY (copy Vivino, invert Untappd/Distiller). Keep the app free forever: no ad clutter, no paywalled rating precision, no subscription gate on discovery. Earn on affiliate/commission from retailer & brewery-direct sales, delivery (Tavour-style daily drops done right), sponsored-but-labeled brewery placements, and anonymized taste-data insights. This directly attacks Untappd's most-hated moves (ads, Insiders paywall, granular-rating gate) and its $599->$1,199 business price gouge.

FIX THE TRUST PROBLEM WITH A STYLE-NORMALIZED, PERSONALIZED SCORE. The industry's biggest unmet need is a score people believe. Rate beers relative to their own style and surface a 'predicted-for-YOU' match (Distiller-style flavor graph + Pintley-style taste model) so a great Kolsch isn't punished against Double IPAs and users stop 1-starring styles they dislike. A credible, personalized score is a defensible moat Untappd structurally cannot retrofit without invalidating its legacy numbers.

GO MULTI-BEVERAGE AND NA-FIRST BY DESIGN, NOT DEFENSE. Build one unified tasting archive across craft beer, NA beer, hop water, cider, RTDs and seltzers from day one (the Drinkist thesis), positioned around the mindful-drinking / drink-less macro trend. This turns Untappd's clumsy, resented 2025 category bolt-on into your native advantage and widens the TAM beyond the shrinking <1% hardcore-ticker base.

WIN GLOBAL BY DESIGN AND SEED THE COLD-START WITH IMPORTED DATA + SCANNING. Untappd, BeerAdvocate and the upstarts are US/English-centric; RateBeer's shutdown and BeerAdvocate's stagnation leave orphaned international data and users. Launch multi-language, offer one-tap import of Untappd/RateBeer history (Taphouse's data-portability hook), and use barcode/label scanning to bootstrap catalog coverage in under-served markets where no incumbent has depth.

COURT VENUES AND BREWERIES AS ALLIES, NOT ATMs. The brewery revolt against Untappd's price hikes and locked-in menus is a distribution gift: offer breweries free or radically cheaper white-labeled digital menus, real ownership of their data and reviews, and Brewlytics-style taste-segmented release alerts to nearby matched drinkers. Free menus for venues = built-in consumer acquisition funnel and a two-sided network Untappd is actively eroding.
risks: COLD-START / NETWORK EFFECTS: Untappd's value is its 15-year check-in graph and BeerAdvocate's legacy library; a rating/social app is worthless empty. Mitigate with aggressive Untappd/RateBeer data import, scan-first solo utility (works with zero friends), and venue-menu seeding — but this is the dominant execution risk.

COMMERCE MONETIZATION IS HARD AND REGULATED: alcohol e-commerce faces a patchwork of shipping laws (US three-tier system, state DTC bans, age verification, EU/UK/global variance). Vivino's ~15% commission model took years and heavy retailer plumbing; a 'free + commission' app has no revenue until commerce actually converts, and 'global' multiplies the legal/logistics burden.

BIG-BEER AND INCUMBENT RETALIATION: AB InBev (ZX Ventures) already bought and killed RateBeer; Next Glass owns Untappd AND BeerAdvocate and could copy a free-tier or cut Business pricing to defend. A well-capitalized incumbent can neutralize a pure-feature wedge, so the moat must be trust + UX + commerce rails, not just 'free.'

MINDFUL-DRINKING TREND CUTS BOTH WAYS: the same demographic shift that opens the NA opportunity is shrinking the core heavy-drinker segment that generates the most check-ins and the most alcohol commission revenue — the highest-value commerce users may be the ones drinking less.

RATING CREDIBILITY IS A DOUBLE-EDGED SWORD: a style-normalized/personalized score is the differentiator, but if it can be gamed or feels like a black box it inherits the same distrust that sank Untappd's number; brewery fear of low ratings hurting sales (a documented Untappd grievance) applies to any new rating platform too.
data.details:
SINGLE CLEAREST WEDGE (bottom line): Build the FREE, global "Vivino for beer" — a scan-first app that fuses (1) Vivino's frictionless AI label/barcode scan-and-rate UX, (2) a trustworthy, style-normalized, personalized taste-match score (fixing the #1 reason people distrust Untappd), and (3) an end-to-end path from discovery -> "where to drink/buy near me" -> in-app purchase/delivery. Give away everything the incumbents paywall (rating precision, discovery, ads-free experience, venue menus) and monetize COMMERCE like Vivino (affiliate/commission, delivery, sponsored-labeled placements, data), not the community like Untappd/Distiller. Make it multi-beverage and NA-first by design to ride the mindful-drinking wave the incumbent is fighting.

WHY NOW: The competitive set has cracked open. RateBeer is dead (AB InBev killed it, Feb 2025). BeerAdvocate is consolidated under Untappd's parent (Next Glass) and stagnant. Untappd itself is 2.3/5 on Trustpilot, ad-heavy, buggy, paywalls rating precision via Insiders ($5.99/mo), doubled its Business price to $1,199/yr with no grandfathering, and made a resented defensive expansion beyond beer in late 2025. Meanwhile Vivino (wine) proves the scan-and-commerce model at scale (70M downloads, 2.7B scans, ~$489M GMV, 15% take rate) and 2024-26 upstarts (Drinkist, Brewver, Pint Please, Brewlytics) are independently converging on exactly this thesis — validating the gap without yet owning it. No single app today unifies trustworthy taste-match + discovery + where-to-get-it + purchase, for free, globally, across beverages.

PER-COMPETITOR SNAPSHOT:
- Untappd (incumbent): check-in + badges gamification; monetizes Insiders subscription + Untappd for Business SaaS. Does well: catalog scale, badges/social habit, venue menu network. Frustrations: ads, bugs, paywalled precision, spammy email, distrusted ratings, brewery price revolt, resented multi-category bolt-on. GAP: free + trustworthy + commerce.
- BeerAdvocate: legacy library/forums/magazine (est. 1996), now Next Glass-owned. Does well: depth, editorial credibility. Frustrations: dated forum UX, not mobile-first. GAP: modern mobile UX over authoritative content.
- RateBeer: SHUT DOWN Feb 2025 (AB InBev/ZX Ventures neglect + craft boycott). GAP: orphaned users/data to capture.
- Distiller (spirits UX ref): crowd Flavor Tags -> auto Flavor Profile, Flavor Search, TruePrice valuation; Pro $4.99/mo paywalls discovery. Emulate the flavor-graph + collection valuation; avoid paywalling discovery.
- Vivino (gold standard): scan label -> instant rating/notes/price -> buy; commission-based commerce (~15%), ~$489M GMV, Premium/Club/ads/data. Emulate the scan-first + commerce model; exploit its all-vintages-averaged score and newly-paywalled list scanning.
- Pintley: free taste-based beer recs; low momentum. Emulate personalization; it never built commerce or scan UX.
- BeerMenus: venue tap-list discovery + alerts, ~7.3M users, single-purpose. Emulate "what's on near me"; it has no rating/taste/commerce layer.
- Tavour: daily-drop rare-beer e-commerce (600+ breweries, flat $16.90 shipping). Beloved product; frustrations = shipping/courier failures, sold-out notifications, CS/refund issues. Emulate curated drops; execute logistics better.
- 2024-26 upstarts: Drinkist (unified multi-beverage AI-scan journal, anti-Untappd positioning), Brewver/Pint Please/Taphouse (data portability), Brewlytics (POS-driven taste profiles + Release Radar alerts for venues). They confirm the wedge but none yet own free + global + commerce + trustworthy-score together.

MACRO: No/low-alcohol +~4%/yr (no-alcohol +7%) 2024-28; US NA >$1B off-premise 2025; ~49% of Americans drinking less in 2025 (+44% since 2023); 27% of 21-29s drink NA weekly; social media is the top NA-discovery channel. A free, global, NA-first, socially-shareable beverage app is aligned with where the market is heading, not where Untappd is defending.

key_findings: The five segments split into two structurally different user types who must coexist in one product: high-frequency loggers/collectors (craft enthusiasts, homebrewers, tickers) who are the retention engine, and occasion-driven social users (students, golfers, casual drinkers) who drive viral reach but log sporadically. Untappd proves the model works at scale: ~8-9M users and ~1 billion lifetime check-ins as of 2025, but with a heavy long-tail of dormant accounts (source: arxiv 2601.04841v1; en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Untappd).

SEGMENT 1 - College students / fraternities / tailgaters. Core motivation: belonging and social proof; drinking is framed as the primary path to connection and 'fitting in' on campus, concentrated in Greek life and tailgate/party culture (renewhealth.com; ncbi PMC9652738). BUT this is the most at-risk-of-churn AND the most brand-sensitive cohort: Gen Z drinks LESS (only ~half of Gen Z drinks at all; 65% planned to drink less in 2025; 39% dry all year - joinreframeapp.com, leger360.com), prefers light lagers/pilsners and fruity/sweet over hoppy craft, and is loyalty-skeptical ('earned, not assumed'). Killer feature: group/crew mode - a live shared session for a tailgate or party (a 'round' everyone checks into, group leaderboard, party photo wall) that turns logging into a group bonding ritual rather than solo journaling. Occasions: tailgates, game-days, house/frat parties, spring break, bar crawls, 21st birthdays. CHURN RISK: feels like a solo 'beer diary' with nobody they know on it; any friction or 21+ signup wall at a party; anything that reads as encouraging binge (reputationally toxic and Gen Z is health-conscious).

SEGMENT 2 - Golfers (beverage-cart culture). Core motivation: the round is a social occasion with drinking baked in - Wisconsin golfers average 5.2 beers/round, Florida 4.8 (heavy.com); a bev cart can do $1,000-5,000/day (vinepair.com, vantagevehicle.com). 2025 trend is golf going younger, looser and more social (music carts, relaxed dress, scrambles, night golf - caddybar.com). Killer feature: on-course integration - tie the check-in to the hole/course and let a foursome run a shared 'scramble scorecard + beer count' (this is exactly the whitespace between beer apps and golf social apps like 18Birdies, which has 2M+ MAU and runs Skins/Nassau/scramble side-games but does NOT track beer). Mobile bev-cart ordering by QR at the tee is an emerging behavior to plug into. Occasions: weekend rounds, corporate/charity scrambles, member-guest, 19th-hole. CHURN RISK: no offline/spotty-signal support on the back nine; nothing golf-native so it stays a generic logger they forget at the clubhouse.

SEGMENT 3 - Serious craft enthusiasts / tickers / homebrewers. Core motivation: mastery, completionism and status - 'ticking' every unique beer, chasing rare/whale releases, building a lifetime tasting journal, and peer credibility. This is the retention core and the group that drives app discourse ('most app updates and user discourses revolve around new badges' - arxiv 2601.04841v1). They already live in Untappd, BeerAdvocate, and homebrew tools like Brewfather (4.9 stars, thousands of ratings) and BeerSmith. Killer feature: a deep, trustworthy database + a rich personal collection/'cellar' with detailed ratings, style breakdowns, badge/level progression, and rare-beer alerts; for homebrewers, the ability to log and share their OWN brews and get feedback. Occasions: bottle shares, brewery pilgrimages, festival ticking, new-release Fridays, homebrew club nights. CHURN RISK: bad/incomplete beer data, duplicate entries, or a shallow rating model; over-simplification for the casual crowd that dilutes their 'serious' tool; and (increasingly) discomfort with gamification that feels like it's pushing volume over discernment.

SEGMENT 4 - Casual social drinkers. Core motivation: low-stakes fun and connection, not collection; they drink 'occasionally' and increasingly 'on their terms' (39% of Gen Z drink only occasionally; intentional moderation rising - opeepl.com, circana.com). They want to remember a good beer, see what friends are into, and get a recommendation - without homework. Killer feature: frictionless one-tap check-in + smart, taste-based recommendations ('you liked X, try Y nearby'), plus a genuinely useful NA/low-ABV surface (Untappd itself expanded from 450 to 900+ categories including NA, THC seltzers, wine in 2025 - thedrinksbusiness.com - precisely to hold this cohort). Occasions: dinner out, patio/happy hour, casual weekends, travel, watching sports at a bar. CHURN RISK: complexity and 'ticker culture' intimidation; a feed full of gatekeeping or 5,000-badge power users; feeling judged for drinking little or choosing NA.

SEGMENT 5 - Brewery owners & taproom staff (the supply side of the marketplace). Core motivation: fill seats, move high-margin taproom pours (taproom margins run 40-50% higher than distribution - ekos/goekos.com), reach nearby drinkers, and get real-time demand data. Untappd for Business monetizes exactly this: ~20,000 venues across 75+ countries, digital/QR menus that auto-sync, insights/analytics, promotions (Total Wine cited a 10x ROI), and in 2025 they added a website builder (lounge.untappd.com; brewbound.com). Killer feature: a self-serve menu/tap-list manager with live analytics (what's checked in, ratings, who's nearby) plus the ability to push events/new-release alerts and run promos to the drinker base - the supply side is what makes the app free-to-the-drinker economically viable. CHURN RISK for venues: thin local user density (no drinkers checking in = no value), clunky menu tooling, or fees that outweigh a small taproom's budget (the 'pint-a-day' price framing exists precisely to keep this low).

HOW THE SEGMENTS CONNECT: friends graph + leaderboards + shared occasions are the connective tissue. Enthusiasts and homebrewers create the content and the trustworthy database; casual and student users consume recommendations and get pulled in via friends' check-ins and group sessions; golfers and tailgaters generate high-volume group occasions; breweries close the loop by turning check-in demand into events, menus and promos. Gamification (badges, streaks, levels, toasts) is the proven cross-segment glue but is double-edged (see risks). Group/crew mechanics (a shared live session for a tailgate, party, or scramble) are the biggest whitespace to bridge the social occasions with the logging habit.

AGE-GATING & RESPONSIBLE DRINKING are non-negotiable and a live reputational fault line. Apple requires alcohol apps to be rated 17+ with an age-restriction mechanism based on verified or declared age; apps that 'encourage excessive consumption of alcohol' or target minors are rejected (developer.apple.com). A 2026 academic ethics analysis of Untappd (arxiv 2601.04841v1) specifically flags its ABV badges (rewarding >10% beers), quantity badges (e.g., 12 beers/day), frequency/streak badges, and time-of-day badges (morning drinking) as design that 'prioritizes engagement over well-being,' and calls humorous disclaimers 'symbolic rather than deterrents.' For a NEW global app in 2026, this is both a compliance requirement and a differentiation opportunity: robust age verification, no volume/ABV-glorifying mechanics, first-class NA/low-ABV support, and genuine moderation nudges align with where Gen Z (the future user base) actually is.
recommendations: Build for two loops in one app: a deep 'collector' loop (rich database, ratings, cellar, badges/levels) to retain enthusiasts/tickers/homebrewers, and a lightweight 'social occasion' loop (one-tap check-in, group/crew sessions, photo walls) to acquire and activate students, golfers and casual drinkers. Do not force casual users through the collector's complexity.

Ship a 'Crew / Live Session' feature as the flagship cross-segment differentiator: a shared, real-time group check-in for tailgates, parties, and golf scrambles, with a group leaderboard and shared media. This is the clearest whitespace - Untappd's social layer is 'limited to basic check-ins and toasts,' and golf social apps track games but not beer.

Win golfers with genuine on-course nativeness: hole/course tagging, a combined 'scramble scorecard + beer tally' for a foursome, offline check-ins that sync later (back-nine dead zones), and hooks into QR bev-cart ordering. Pursue partnership/integration with a golf-scoring app (e.g., 18Birdies-style) rather than rebuilding golf scoring from scratch.

Treat the beer database as the moat for the enthusiast segment - invest early in data completeness, de-duplication, and a rich rating model, and let homebrewers log and share their own recipes/brews. Poor data is the #1 churn driver for serious users. Support the 2025 category expansion reality (NA, low-ABV, seltzers) so the database stays relevant as tastes shift.

Make the brewery/taproom side self-serve and high-value from day one, because the supply side subsidizes 'free' for drinkers: live tap-list/menu manager, QR menus, check-in analytics, event and new-release push, and low/tiered pricing framed for small taprooms. Solve the cold-start locally (density per venue) before expanding a market, since low drinker density kills venue value.

Design responsible-drinking IN, not bolted on: age-verify (17+ / 21+ per Apple and local law), avoid badges/mechanics that reward volume, ABV, streaks or morning drinking, surface NA/low-ABV prominently, and add real moderation features (pace tracking, 'skip a round' framing). This is simultaneously compliance, churn-prevention for health-conscious Gen Z, and defense against the exact ethics critique leveled at Untappd.

Anchor acquisition on the friends graph and shared occasions, not solo journaling. A lone user with no friends on the app churns; seed density around campuses, golf clubs, and breweries so new users immediately see people they know checking in.
risks: Gen Z's structural decline in drinking (only ~half drink; 65% intended to drink less in 2025) means the future core of every consumer segment is smaller and more moderation-minded than the millennial craft wave that built Untappd - a beer-only, volume-celebrating app risks aging into a shrinking base. Mitigation: first-class NA/low-ABV and social-occasion framing over consumption volume.

Reputational and regulatory exposure: gamification that rewards quantity/ABV/streaks can be read as 'encouraging excessive consumption,' risking App Store rejection (17+ rules) and public/academic criticism (the 2026 Untappd ethics paper is a template for that critique). A single 'binge badge' controversy could sink a consumer alcohol app's trust.

Two-sided cold-start: the app is only valuable to breweries with enough local drinkers and only valuable to drinkers with enough friends/venues - launching 'global' thinly everywhere risks being valuable nowhere. Density must be built market-by-market.

Segment dilution: over-simplifying for casual/student users can alienate the enthusiast retention core (who see it as their serious tool), while over-complicating for enthusiasts scares off casual users. The product must visibly serve both without one experience swamping the other.

Golfer and tailgater usage is spiky and offline-prone (dead zones, one big day then nothing); without offline support and group hooks these users check in once and never return. Occasion-only users have inherently weak retention unless the social graph pulls them back.

Loyalty is fragile and earned: Gen Z frequently switches brands even mid-outing and won't tolerate friction, gatekeeping, or feeling judged - churn can be immediate and permanent if early experience is clunky or the community feels elitist.
sources: https://arxiv.org/html/2601.04841v1 (A Longitudinal Analysis of Gamification in Untappd: Ethical Reflections on a Social Drinking Application, 2026)

AUDIENCE PROFILE - FREE GLOBAL BEER APP (2026)

The audience splits into two structural user types that must coexist: (A) high-frequency loggers/collectors = the retention engine (craft enthusiasts, tickers, homebrewers), and (B) occasion-driven social users = the viral reach (students/tailgaters, golfers, casual drinkers). Breweries are the supply side that subsidizes "free." Untappd validates the model (~8-9M users, ~1B lifetime check-ins) but its social layer is thin and its gamification is now under explicit ethics fire.

--- SEGMENT 1: COLLEGE STUDENTS / FRATERNITIES / TAILGATERS ---
Core motivation: belonging + social proof. On US campuses, drinking is framed as the main route to connection; concentrated in Greek life, tailgates, party culture. Killer feature: CREW / LIVE SESSION mode - a shared group check-in for a tailgate or party (group leaderboard, shared photo wall, a "round" everyone joins) that makes logging a bonding ritual, not a solo diary. Occasions: tailgates, game-days, frat/house parties, spring break, bar crawls, 21st birthdays. Churn: solo experience with no friends on it; any signup/age wall friction at a party; anything that reads as pushing binge (Gen Z is health-conscious and reputationally allergic to it). Nuance: Gen Z drinks LESS and prefers light lagers/pilsners/fruity over hoppy craft; loyalty is "earned, not assumed."

--- SEGMENT 2: GOLFERS (BEVERAGE-CART CULTURE) ---
Core motivation: the round is a drinking-social occasion (WI 5.2 beers/round, FL 4.8; bev carts do $1-5k/day). 2025 golf is going younger/looser/more social (music carts, scrambles, night golf). Killer feature: ON-COURSE INTEGRATION - hole/course tagging + a combined "scramble scorecard + beer tally" for a foursome, offline check-ins that sync (back-nine dead zones), and QR bev-cart ordering hooks. This is whitespace: golf social apps (18Birdies, 2M+ MAU; GameBook) run Skins/Nassau/scramble but track no beer. Occasions: weekend rounds, corporate/charity scrambles, member-guest, 19th hole. Churn: no offline support; nothing golf-native so it's forgotten at the clubhouse.

--- SEGMENT 3: SERIOUS CRAFT ENTHUSIASTS / TICKERS / HOMEBREWERS ---
Core motivation: mastery + completionism + status - "ticking" every unique beer, chasing whales, a lifetime journal, peer credibility. Retention core; drives app discourse (updates revolve around badges). Already in Untappd/BeerAdvocate/Brewfather(4.9*)/BeerSmith. Killer feature: DEEP TRUSTWORTHY DATABASE + rich personal cellar (detailed ratings, style breakdowns, levels/badges, rare-release alerts); homebrewers log and share their OWN brews for feedback. Occasions: bottle shares, brewery pilgrimages, festival ticking, new-release Fridays, homebrew club nights. Churn: bad/duplicate data, shallow ratings, over-simplification for casuals diluting their "serious" tool, and growing discomfort with volume-over-discernment gamification.

--- SEGMENT 4: CASUAL SOCIAL DRINKERS ---
Core motivation: low-stakes fun + connection, not collection. Drink "occasionally," increasingly on their own terms; moderation rising. Killer feature: FRICTIONLESS one-tap check-in + taste-based recs ("you liked X, try Y nearby") + a real NA/low-ABV surface (Untappd went 450->900+ categories incl. NA/THC/wine in 2025 to hold exactly this cohort). Occasions: dinner out, happy hour/patio, casual weekends, travel, sports at a bar. Churn: ticker-culture intimidation, a gatekeeping feed, feeling judged for drinking little or choosing NA.

--- SEGMENT 5: BREWERY OWNERS & TAPROOM STAFF (SUPPLY SIDE) ---
Core motivation: fill seats, move high-margin taproom pours (40-50% higher margin than distribution), reach nearby drinkers, get live demand data. Untappd for Business is the reference: ~20,000 venues in 75+ countries, auto-syncing digital/QR menus, insights/analytics, promos (Total Wine 10x ROI), + a 2025 website builder, all for ~"a pint a day." Killer feature: SELF-SERVE MENU/TAP-LIST MANAGER with live check-in analytics + event/new-release push + promos to the drinker base. Churn (venue side): thin local user density (no check-ins = no value), clunky tooling, fees a small taproom can't justify.

--- HOW THE SEGMENTS CONNECT ---
Friends graph + leaderboards + shared occasions are the glue. Enthusiasts/homebrewers create content + the trustworthy DB; casual/student users consume recs and get pulled in via friends' check-ins and group sessions; golfers/tailgaters generate high-volume group occasions; breweries convert that demand into events/menus/promos, closing the loop and funding "free." Gamification is proven cross-segment glue but double-edged. The single biggest bridging whitespace is a shared LIVE GROUP SESSION (tailgate / party / scramble).

--- RESPONSIBLE DRINKING & AGE-GATING (NON-NEGOTIABLE) ---
Apple mandates 17+ rating + an age-restriction mechanism (verified or declared age); apps that "encourage excessive consumption of alcohol" or target minors are rejected. A 2026 ethics analysis of Untappd specifically condemns ABV badges (>10% beers), quantity badges (12 beers/day), frequency/streak badges, and time-of-day (morning) badges as engagement-over-wellbeing design, calling humorous disclaimers "symbolic rather than deterrents." For a new 2026 app this is both compliance and differentiation: robust age verification, NO volume/ABV-glorifying mechanics, first-class NA/low-ABV, and genuine moderation nudges - which also happen to match where Gen Z (the future user base) already is.

key_findings: UNTAPPD IS THE TRAP. The public Untappd API is technically the only source that natively has both crowd check-ins AND per-beer ratings AND 'popular near me' signal — but its API Terms of Use structurally forbid the exact product being scoped. Term 14: 'You cannot use the Untappd data to build your own beer database.' Term 13: caches must be deleted every 24h (no persistent catalog). Term 15: no mining/analytics ('popular beers near me' IS analytics). Term 5: no selling/renting/trading data. Rate limit is 100 calls/hour/key — unusable for a location-fanout consumer app. It is effectively partner/BD-only for a real product; the self-serve key exists but its ToS make a ratings-driven catalog app non-compliant. Do NOT build the hero feature on Untappd's public API. (untappd.com/terms/api, untappd.com/api/docs)

VENUE TAP LISTS ARE WALLED OFF. Untappd tap/menu data is explicitly 'Untappd for Business' (UTFB Premium) only — the public API doc states 'Venue Beer List (or tap list information) are NOT SUPPORTED in the Public API.' So the cleanest 'what's actually pouring here right now' source is a paid B2B product aimed at venues, not app developers, and requires a commercial agreement. (docs.business.untappd.com, help.untappd.com UTFB API article)

BreweryDB (the old commercial one, brewerydb.com) is DEAD to the public — 'no longer providing public access,' invites data-partnership discussion only. Its spiritual open successor, OPEN BREWERY DB (openbrewerydb.org), is genuinely free: no API key, no signup, no rate limits, GitHub-maintained, but it is BREWERY-DIRECTORY ONLY. Fields: brewery name, type (micro/nano/regional/brewpub/etc.), address, city/state/postal/country, lat/long, phone, website. It has ZERO individual beers, ZERO ratings, ZERO check-ins. Great for the POI/brewery layer, useless for the beer catalog or ratings. Endpoints: list (with by_dist lat/long, by_city, by_state, by_type), single, random, search, autocomplete, metadata. (openbrewerydb.org/documentation, openbrewerydb.org/faq)

PUNK API is a museum piece, not a catalog. It's BrewDog's DIY Dog homebrew recipe set — the original punkapi.com was discontinued May 1 2024; a community rehost exists (punkapi-alxiw.amvera.io/v3) with exactly 415 beers + recipes/images/ABV/IBU. It is one brewery's back-catalog, not a live commercial beer database, and has no ratings or geo. Fine for demo/seed/test data only. (punkapi.com, github.com/alxiw/punkapi)

FOURSQUARE OS PLACES is the sleeper win for cold-start POI. Foursquare open-sourced its 100M+ global POI dataset under APACHE 2.0 (commercial use OK, permissive, no share-alike), 22 core attributes, 1000+ categories (includes Bar/Brewery/Brewpub/Beer Bar), monthly refresh, distributed as GeoParquet on S3 (s3://fsq-os-places-us-east-1/), Hugging Face (foursquare/fsq-os-places), and Snowflake. This lets you OWN a brewery+bar POI table outright with no per-call fees and no caching restrictions — the opposite of Google/Untappd. Also folded into Overture Maps (Places theme, ~75M+ POIs; Foursquare-sourced rows are Apache 2.0, others CDLA 2.0). (opensource.foursquare.com/os-places, huggingface.co/datasets/foursquare/fsq-os-places, docs.overturemaps.org/guides/places)

GOOGLE PLACES = expensive + un-cacheable. 2026 pricing: Nearby/Text Search and Place Details Pro ~$32/1,000; the moment you request ratings/hours/phone it jumps to Enterprise ~$35/1,000, and reviews/atmosphere ~$40/1,000 (billed at the most expensive field in your mask). Critically, Google ToS PROHIBIT caching/storing Places content — ONLY the place_id may be stored indefinitely. So you cannot build a persistent 'popular beers near me' index off Google ratings; you'd re-pay per view and still can't legally warehouse it. Good for live enrichment/validation, bad as your system of record. (developers.google.com/maps/documentation/places/web-service/policies + usage-and-billing)

APPLE MapKit / Apple Maps Server API is free for commercial apps and has POI categories (incl. brewery), attractive if the app is iOS-native (per the user's Lore/Nalee stack, they ship native Swift). But Apple's POI results are meant for display/search within Apple Maps, similarly not a warehouse-able beer/ratings source, and coverage/permission to persist is restrictive. Good as the on-device map + 'nearby venue' layer on iOS; not your ratings source. (developer.apple.com/maps, Apple Maps Server API docs)

OPEN FOOD FACTS is a real, overlooked cold-start source for the BEER PRODUCT catalog + label images. Free, no key, barcode lookup (world.openfoodfacts.org/api/v2), 4M+ products incl. beers with brand/name/images. License is ODbL (database) + Database Contents License + images CC-BY-SA — SHARE-ALIKE/attribution obligations apply, so it shapes how you can redistribute, but it's legal to build on. Lets you seed beer identities and get label images by scanning a can/bottle. (world.openfoodfacts.org/data, openfoodfacts.github.io API docs)
recommendations: POWER 'popular beers near me' WITHOUT Untappd — generate the signal yourself from first-party check-ins. There is no compliant third-party API that hands you 'top beers at this venue right now.' The three architectural options are: (a) CROWD CHECK-INS = your own users check in a beer at a venue (venue from Foursquare/Apple POI, beer from your catalog); rank beers per geohash/venue by recent check-in velocity + rating. This is the Untappd model but on YOUR data, so no ToS problem. (b) VENUE MENUS = ingest tap lists (hard: Untappd-for-Business is the incumbent and it's paid/venue-side; BeerMenus/TapHunter-style scraping is fragile and legally gray). (c) POI + user ratings = show nearby breweries/bars (Foursquare/Apple) and let users rate the venue's beers. RECOMMENDATION: ship (a)+(c) — own the check-in graph; treat menus as a later premium/partnership layer. The hero feature must be a derived metric over first-party data, not a proxied third-party feed.

COLD-START in three moves so the map is never empty on day one: (1) POI layer — bulk-load Foursquare OS Places (Apache 2.0) filtered to bar/brewery/beer-bar/brewpub categories into your own Postgres+PostGIS; enrich with Open Brewery DB for brewery-specific fields (type, brewery website). Both are free and warehouse-able. (2) BEER CATALOG seed — seed from Open Food Facts (barcode→beer+brand+label image) + Punk API 415-beer set for demo polish; grow via user-generated additions (scan-to-add). (3) RATINGS bootstrap — you start at zero ratings, so gate the empty state with editorializ­ed 'staff picks'/style-based defaults and aggressively incentivize the first check-ins (the classic marketplace cold-start). Do NOT import Untappd ratings to fake density — it violates their ToS and poisons your data provenance.

DEDUPE / CANONICAL BEER IDENTITY — this is your hardest data problem, budget for it. Beer names are messy (vintages, collabs, ABV variants, brewery renames). Build a canonical model: brewery_id (canonical, keyed to your POI/Open Brewery DB id) → beer_id (canonical) → beer_alias/variant. Match on normalized (brewery + name + style + ABV) with fuzzy matching (trigram/pg_trgm, or embeddings for name similarity) and a human-review queue for merges. Use BARCODE (GTIN from Open Food Facts / scan) as the strongest natural key when available. Keep source-provenance columns (fsq_id, obdb_id, off_barcode, google_place_id) so you can re-sync and never lose lineage. place_id from Google is store-forever-legal and a good cross-reference key for venues.

IMAGES / LABELS — never hotlink or warehouse Google/Untappd images. Sources: Open Food Facts label images (CC-BY-SA — must attribute + share-alike, so fine for display with credit), Punk API images (BrewDog set), and above all USER-UPLOADED photos (your own, cleanest license via your ToS). Run user uploads through moderation. Store in object storage (Supabase Storage / S3) with a CDN; keep a canonical label image + gallery per beer_id.

LICENSING/ToS GOTCHAS to encode as hard rules in the pipeline: (1) NEVER persist Google Places content except place_id. (2) NEVER build a catalog from Untappd public API — it's a ToS violation and the 100 req/hr limit makes it impossible anyway. (3) Foursquare OS Places Apache 2.0 = safe to own/redistribute (light attribution courtesy). (4) Open Brewery DB = effectively public domain / open, safe. (5) Open Food Facts = ODbL + CC-BY-SA — attribution + share-alike obligations; keep OFF-derived data segregated so share-alike doesn't 'infect' your proprietary tables (store it, attribute it, but design so your first-party ratings/catalog aren't legally entangled). (6) If you ever want real Untappd data, it's a business-development/partnership conversation (Untappd Insiders / UTFB), not a self-serve API — price and gate accordingly.

MVP STACK (concrete): Supabase (Postgres + PostGIS extension + Storage + Auth) — matches the user's existing Nalee/Lore Supabase muscle memory. Tables: venues (seeded from Foursquare OS Places + Open Brewery DB, PostGIS geography col, GiST index), beers (canonical, seeded from Open Food Facts + Punk API), beer_aliases, checkins (user_id, beer_id, venue_id, rating, created_at), and a materialized/edge-computed 'popular_beers_near(lat,lng,radius)' query = check-ins in last N days within ST_DWithin, grouped by beer, scored by count*recency + avg rating. Live POI display on iOS via Apple MapKit (free) to avoid Google per-call fees. Google Places used ONLY as an optional on-demand enrichment/verify call (respecting no-cache). Barcode scan → Open Food Facts for scan-to-add. This whole MVP has near-zero third-party data cost.

SCALE STACK: keep Postgres+PostGIS as source of truth but add (1) a dedicated geo-ranking layer — precompute per-geohash 'trending beers' rollups on a schedule (or use Postgres materialized views / a small OLAP like ClickHouse or DuckDB for the check-in aggregation, since check-ins become high-volume time-series). (2) Refresh Foursquare OS Places monthly (it updates monthly) via a batch job reading the S3/Hugging Face Parquet; diff into your venues table by fsq_id. (3) Search: pg_trgm is fine early; move to a real search index (Typesense/Meilisearch/OpenSearch) for beer+venue autocomplete at scale. (4) For 'menus,' pursue UTFB/venue partnerships or a POS/menu-ingestion deal rather than scraping. (5) Consider Overture Maps for a broader, foundation-backed POI base if you go multi-country. Cost driver at scale is NOT data licensing (you own it) but the geo-aggregation compute and image storage/CDN — which is the right place for cost to live.
risks: Untappd dependency is a legal + technical dead end: building the hero feature on Untappd's public API violates Terms 5/13/14/15 AND the 100 req/hr limit makes location-fanout impossible. Any pitch or plan that assumes 'we'll just pull popular beers from Untappd' should be killed now.

Cold-start ratings chasm: Open Brewery DB, Foursquare OS Places, Punk API, and Open Food Facts give you POIs + a beer catalog + images but ZERO ratings and ZERO check-ins. 'Most popular beers near me' is inherently a data-network-effect feature — it looks empty until you have local user density. Underestimating this is the #1 product risk; the data plan cannot fix it, only the growth/incentive strategy can.

Google Places no-cache rule is easy to violate accidentally: if any engineer stores a Google rating or name into Postgres to power 'popular near me,' that's a ToS breach and the feature is un-warehouse-able anyway. Enforce 'place_id only' at the schema/review level.

Open Food Facts share-alike (ODbL + CC-BY-SA) can create redistribution obligations if OFF-derived catalog data is commingled with proprietary data you later want to license/sell. Segregate and attribute, or a future data-monetization move gets legally messy.

Venue tap-list / menu data has no good open source — it's Untappd-for-Business (paid, venue-side) or fragile scraping (BeerMenus/TapHunter). Promising 'what's on tap right now' in the MVP is high-effort/low-reliability; scope it as a later partnership feature, not a launch guarantee.

Foursquare OS Places is monthly-batch and community-open, so venue freshness (new/closed breweries) lags reality; you'll need a user-correction loop ('this place closed / wrong location') to keep POI quality up — same class of problem the user already knows from Nalee/Lore content integrity.

Beer identity dedupe is genuinely hard and, if skipped, silently destroys the ranking feature (the same beer split across 5 spellings never trends). It needs real engineering + a human merge queue, not a one-off script.
data.details:
FEASIBILITY VERDICT: The app is buildable, but ONLY if 'popular beers near me' is computed from your own first-party check-in graph, not proxied from a third party. No commercially-usable API sells you 'top-rated beers pouring near this location' — Untappd has the data but forbids the use case (and rate-limits it to death), Google has ratings but bans caching, and the open sources have POIs/catalog/images but no ratings.

SOURCE MATRIX (what each is actually good for):
- Open Brewery DB (open, free, no key, no limits) → BREWERY POI layer only. No beers, no ratings. USE for brewery directory/enrichment.
- (old) BreweryDB.com → DEAD to public; partnership-only. IGNORE.
- Untappd public API → has beers+ratings+check-ins+venues BUT ToS forbid building a beer DB / caching >24h / analytics / selling; 100 req/hr. Partner/BD path only (Untappd Insiders, UTFB). DO NOT base the product on it.
- Untappd for Business (UTFB) → the only real tap-list/menu source; paid, venue-side, commercial agreement. Consider as a future menu-data partnership.
- Punk API → 415 BrewDog homebrew recipes+images; discontinued original, community rehost. SEED/DEMO data only.
- Foursquare OS Places → 100M+ POIs, Apache 2.0, GeoParquet on S3/HuggingFace/Snowflake, monthly refresh, includes bar/brewery categories. OWN this as your POI base — the single best cold-start asset. Also available via Overture Maps.
- Google Places → live POI + ratings, but ~$32-40/1k and CANNOT cache anything but place_id. Optional on-demand enrichment only.
- Apple MapKit / Apple Maps Server API → free, native-iOS-friendly POI + map; not a warehouse-able ratings source. USE for the map/nearby layer on iOS.
- Open Food Facts → barcode→beer product + label images, free, ODbL/CC-BY-SA. USE for beer-catalog seed + scan-to-add + label images (mind share-alike).

'POPULAR BEERS NEAR ME' — HOW TO POWER IT: The compliant path is (venue from Foursquare/Apple POI) + (beer from your catalog seeded via Open Food Facts/Punk) + (rating/check-in from YOUR users). Rank = recent check-in count x recency-decay x avg rating within ST_DWithin(user_location, radius). This is Untappd's mechanic rebuilt on data you own, with no third-party ToS exposure.

RECOMMENDED STACK:
- MVP: Supabase (Postgres + PostGIS + Storage + Auth). Seed venues from Foursquare OS Places (Apache 2.0) + Open Brewery DB; seed beers from Open Food Facts + Punk API; Apple MapKit for the iOS map; Google Places only as optional no-cache enrichment. Near-zero third-party data cost. Canonical model: brewery → beer → alias, with source-provenance ids (fsq_id, obdb_id, off_barcode, google_place_id) and pg_trgm/embedding fuzzy dedupe + human merge queue. Barcode/GTIN as strongest natural key.
- Scale: keep PostGIS as SoR; add precomputed per-geohash 'trending' rollups (materialized views or ClickHouse/DuckDB for high-volume check-in aggregation); monthly Foursquare Parquet re-sync; Typesense/Meilisearch for autocomplete; pursue UTFB/POS partnerships for real menus; Overture Maps if going multi-country. Cost lives in geo-compute + image CDN, not data licensing (you own the data).

This aligns with the user's known Supabase-native, iOS-native (Swift) build pattern from Lore/Nalee.

key_findings: The app stays 100% free to consumers; every dollar comes from the supply side (breweries/taprooms, alcohol e-commerce, event organizers, merch) or from a future B2B tier. This is the exact model the category leader Untappd runs: consumers are free, breweries pay $899-$1,199/yr for Untappd for Business plus extra for marketing/sponsored badges. That validates the wedge but leaves room since Untappd's B2B tooling is menu/POS-heavy, not discovery-partnership-heavy.

MARKET SCALE: ~9,578 US breweries in 2025 (Brewers Association), with taprooms + brewpubs = 73% of craft businesses (the on-premise venues most motivated to pay for foot traffic). Breweries typically spend 3-10% of revenue on marketing (startups 12-20%), so a real budget line exists to capture. Untappd itself has ~11M+ registered members historically — that is the ceiling for a beer-discovery social graph, so plan revenue that only activates at tens-of-thousands-to-low-millions of users, not day one.

RANKED FIT (best-first): (1) Brewery/taproom partnerships — featured placement, verified profiles, 'on tap now', event promotion — highest fit, most defensible, tie-house-safe if structured as a paid ad/listing not a kickback. (2) Future B2B 'Pro' tier for breweries (recurring SaaS, the durable engine, natural upsell of #1). (3) Ticketed events & beer-festival affiliate/co-promotion. (4) Sponsored badges & challenges (proven Untappd revenue line). (5) Alcohol e-commerce/delivery affiliate — real but structurally weak: low rates (ReserveBar ~4%, Beerwulf 6-12%, Tavour per-signup), Instacart EXCLUDES alcohol from its affiliate commissions, Drizly is dead (shut 2024). (6) Merch/print-on-demand — smallest, best as brand/community feature not core revenue.

BIGGEST COMPLIANCE CONSTRAINT is tied-house law, not ad content. If the app steers users toward one specific retailer/brewery in exchange for money, a state regulator can treat it as an illegal 'thing of value' to a retailer. Safe structure: sell advertising/placement on a neutral platform to all comers at published rates (a 'digital sign' analogy), keep manufacturer money and retailer money in clearly separate lanes, and don't let a brewery pay to suppress a competitor. This is state-by-state and actively enforced in 2025.

AFFILIATE REALITY CHECK: the delivery-affiliate dream is weaker than it looks. Drizly shut down in 2024. Instacart explicitly excludes alcohol from affiliate commissions (alcohol is handled via separate retail-media deals like Bottlecapps/Carrot Ads, not open affiliate). DoorDash/Uber Eats monetize alcohol through merchant commissions (15-30%), not a public consumer-affiliate deep-link program. That leaves e-commerce retailers (ReserveBar 4%/30-day cookie, Tavour per-signup, Beerwulf 6-12%, BroBasket up to 12%, Athletic Brewing 3%) as the only clean affiliate inventory — useful garnish, not a main course.
recommendations: LEAD WITH BREWERY/TAPROOM PARTNERSHIPS as the flagship revenue line. UX wiring: verified brewery profiles (blue-check trust), 'Featured' placement in map/search/nearby, a live 'On Tap Now' module the brewery self-updates, and event cards in a local feed. Sell it as tiered self-serve advertising (e.g. Free claimed profile / ~$49-99/mo Verified+featured / ~$199-299/mo Premium with event promotion + analytics), benchmarked below Untappd's $899-1,199/yr so you undercut on price. Scale needed: meaningful only once you have real local density — target ~5,000-25,000 MAU in a beachhead metro before pitching; a brewery pays for eyeballs that already exist. Compliance: publish a neutral rate card open to every brewery, frame placement as advertising, never as pay-to-rank-competitors-down.

BUILD THE B2B 'PRO' TIER as the durable engine, sold ONLY to breweries (never consumers). Bundle: analytics dashboard (who's checking in, demographics, dwell, competitor benchmarking), menu/tap-list management, review-response tools, campaign manager for badges/events, and CRM export. Price ~$99-299/mo. UX wiring: a separate authenticated 'for Breweries' web portal that feeds the consumer app. Scale needed: this monetizes the SAME audience as partnerships, so it activates on the same ~10k-MAU-per-metro threshold; land breweries on a free claimed profile first, then upsell to Pro on proven traffic. This is the highest-LTV, most predictable (recurring SaaS) line and the eventual valuation driver.

MONETIZE EVENTS via ticketing affiliate + paid event promotion. UX wiring: an in-app events/festival calendar filtered by location and followed breweries, with 'Get Tickets' deep-links to Eventbrite/DICE/Ticket Fairy (referral/affiliate links or promo-code attribution) and paid 'boosted event' slots breweries buy. Beer-fest ticketing fees run ~$0.39-$4+/ticket, and organizers already reward referral partners with promo-code splits. Scale needed: moderate — works with a few thousand engaged local users because ticket AOV is high; a single festival referral can beat hundreds of low-value affiliate clicks. Compliance: ticketing referrals are age-gated but far cleaner than product-sales referrals; still gate on 21+ and geo.

RUN SPONSORED BADGES & CHALLENGES as a high-margin brand line, mirroring Untappd's proven model. UX wiring: brewery- or festival-sponsored digital badges/achievements users unlock by checking in a style, visiting a taproom, or completing a passport/crawl, with optional prize redemption. Untappd charges a monthly sponsorship fee on top of the base subscription and requires ~1 month lead time — price yours as a campaign add-on (e.g. $299-999/campaign). Scale needed: needs an active check-in loop and enough users that a badge feels earnable — realistically post-50k users. Compliance: no purchase-required framing that looks like paying consumers for consumption; keep it a marketing promotion run by the platform, not the brewery paying users directly.

TREAT ALCOHOL E-COMMERCE AFFILIATE AS GARNISH, not a pillar, and set expectations accordingly. Wire a 'Buy / Ship this beer' or 'Find delivery' button on beer detail pages that routes to whichever retailer is legal + available in the user's state: ReserveBar/Minibar (~4%, 30-day cookie), Tavour (per-signup bounty), Beerwulf (6-12%), BroBasket (up to 12%), Athletic Brewing (3%, NA-friendly and lowest legal risk), plus DoorDash/Uber for on-demand where a partner/retail-media deal can be struck. Do NOT promise Instacart alcohol affiliate revenue (excluded) or Drizly (shut down 2024). Scale needed: pure volume play — negligible under ~100k MAU. Compliance: hard 21+ age-gate, geo-suppress the button in Utah/Delaware (DTC bans) and dry counties, and disclose affiliate relationships per FTC.

ADD MERCH via print-on-demand as a community/brand feature, not a revenue target. Wire a lightweight app/brewery-branded store (Printful/Printify) where breweries can sell their own merch through their profile (you take a small platform cut) and you sell app-branded gear to superfans. POD margins ~20-40% with no inventory risk. Scale needed: only worthwhile once you have a passionate core (post-100k users) — keep it optional so it never distracts from the partnership/Pro engine. Compliance: merch is not alcohol, so it sidesteps most beverage-ad law — the cleanest line legally.

COMPLIANCE OPERATING RULES to bake into product from day one: (1) 21+ age-gate at signup and before any purchase/delivery CTA. (2) Include TTB-mandated advertising info where required — 2024 guidance now lets you satisfy mandatory statements via a LinkTree-style link, and you are responsible for influencer/UGC compliance too. (3) Keep manufacturer money and retailer money in separate lanes to avoid tied-house 'thing of value' violations — sell neutral, published-rate placement, never pay-to-bury-competitors. (4) Geo-suppress alcohol-commerce features in ban states (Utah, Delaware) and dry counties; alcohol laws are state-by-state and 'more fragmented and actively enforced than ever' in 2025. (5) FTC affiliate disclosure on every referral.
risks: Tied-house / 'thing of value' exposure: if a paid featured-placement or delivery-referral is construed as the app steering consumers to a specific retailer for money, a state ABC can deem it an illegal inducement. Mitigate by selling neutral platform advertising at published rates open to all, not per-retailer kickbacks. This is the single biggest structural/legal risk and it varies by state.

Affiliate revenue is thinner than founders expect: Drizly is dead (2024), Instacart excludes alcohol from affiliate commissions, DoorDash/Uber don't offer a public consumer-affiliate deep-link, and the retailers that DO pay (ReserveBar 4%, Athletic 3%) have low rates. Anyone modeling delivery-affiliate as a core pillar will be disappointed; it only works at 100k+ MAU as garnish.

Chicken-and-egg on the two best lines: brewery partnerships and the Pro tier both require pre-existing local user density to be sellable. If the app can't hit ~10k MAU in a beachhead metro, there is nothing to monetize — revenue is fundamentally back-loaded and gated on consumer growth, so runway must cover a long free period.

Category headwind: US craft beer contracted in 2025 (breweries down 218 to 9,578; new openings fell from 518 to 300; 481 closures). A shrinking, cost-pressured brewery base is a harder B2B customer — marketing budgets get cut first in a downturn. Pricing must stay clearly under Untappd's $899-1,199/yr to win share.

Incumbent moat: Untappd owns the beer-discovery social graph, the check-in habit, and existing B2B billing relationships (menus/POS). Every proposed line competes with something Untappd already sells; differentiation has to come from a better discovery UX, lower price, or a niche Untappd underserves (events, local crawls, delivery routing).

Geo/legal patchwork operational cost: age-gating, dry-county and ban-state (Utah, Delaware) suppression, TTB mandatory-statement handling, and FTC disclosure are ongoing engineering + legal overhead. Getting one state wrong on alcohol commerce can trigger enforcement, so the compliance layer is a real, recurring cost — not a one-time checkbox.

MONETIZATION STRATEGY FOR A FREE-TO-USER BEER/BREWERY APP (2026)

Core principle: users never pay. All revenue is supply-side (breweries, retailers, event organizers, merch) + a future B2B SaaS tier. This mirrors the proven Untappd model (consumers free; breweries pay for Untappd for Business + marketing).

=== RANKED BY FIT ===

1) BREWERY / TAPROOM PARTNERSHIPS (BEST FIT)
- What: verified profiles, featured/nearby placement, live "On Tap Now", event promotion cards.
- UX: claimed free profile -> upsell to Verified (~$49-99/mo) -> Premium (~$199-299/mo) with event promo + analytics. Undercut Untappd for Business ($899/yr Essentials, $1,199/yr Premium).
- Scale: ~5k-25k MAU in a beachhead metro (density before you can sell eyeballs).
- Compliance: neutral published rate card, framed as advertising/"digital sign", not pay-to-rank-down-competitors. Tied-house safe if manufacturer and retailer money stay in separate lanes.
- Why #1: defensible, recurring, and the whole app already produces the inventory (local attention).

2) B2B "PRO" TIER (DURABLE ENGINE)
- Brewery-only SaaS: analytics, tap-list/menu mgmt, review response, campaign manager, competitor benchmarking. ~$99-299/mo. Separate "for Breweries" portal.
- Scale: same audience as #1; activates at the same density. Highest LTV, most predictable, the eventual valuation driver.

3) TICKETED EVENTS & BEER-FEST AFFILIATE
- In-app events/festival calendar; "Get Tickets" deep-links to Eventbrite/DICE/Ticket Fairy (referral or promo-code attribution) + paid "boosted event" slots.
- Economics: fest ticketing fees ~$0.39-$4+/ticket; organizers already split via promo codes. High AOV -> one referral beats hundreds of affiliate clicks.
- Scale: moderate (a few thousand engaged locals). Cleaner legally than product sales (still 21+/geo gate).

4) SPONSORED BADGES & CHALLENGES (PROVEN LINE)
- Brewery/festival-sponsored digital badges unlocked by check-ins, taproom visits, passports/crawls; optional prize redemption.
- Untappd charges a monthly sponsorship fee + ~1 month lead time; price yours as a $299-999 campaign add-on.
- Scale: needs an active check-in loop; realistically post-50k users.
- Compliance: platform-run promotion, never brewery-paying-users-to-drink.

5) ALCOHOL E-COMMERCE / DELIVERY AFFILIATE (GARNISH, NOT PILLAR)
- "Buy / Ship / Find delivery" button routing to the legal, in-state retailer: ReserveBar/Minibar ~4% (30-day cookie), Tavour per-signup, Beerwulf 6-12%, BroBasket up to 12%, Athletic Brewing 3% (NA, lowest legal risk); DoorDash/Uber where a retail-media/partner deal exists.
- REALITY: Drizly shut down 2024; Instacart EXCLUDES alcohol from affiliate commissions (separate retail-media via Bottlecapps/Carrot Ads); DoorDash/Uber monetize alcohol via 15-30% merchant commissions, not a public consumer-affiliate deep-link. So affiliate = low-rate e-commerce only.
- Scale: pure volume; negligible under ~100k MAU.
- Compliance: 21+ gate; suppress in Utah & Delaware (DTC bans) and dry counties; FTC disclosure.

6) MERCH / PRINT-ON-DEMAND (SMALLEST)
- Printful/Printify store: breweries sell their merch via profile (platform cut) + app-branded superfan gear. Margins ~20-40%, no inventory. Post-100k users. Legally cleanest (not alcohol).

=== MARKET SIZING ===
~9,578 US breweries (2025, down 218 YoY; new openings 518->300; 481 closures). Taprooms+brewpubs = 73% of craft businesses = the venues most motivated to pay for foot traffic. Breweries spend 3-10% of revenue on marketing (startups 12-20%). Untappd historically ~11M+ registered members = social-graph ceiling. Craft contracted in 2025 -> price under Untappd, expect budget sensitivity.

=== COMPLIANCE RULEBOOK (build in day one) ===
- 21+ age-gate at signup and before any purchase/delivery CTA.
- TTB 2024 guidance: mandatory ad statements can be satisfied via a LinkTree-style link; platform is responsible for influencer/UGC compliance.
- Tied-house: keep manufacturer $ and retailer $ in separate lanes; sell neutral, published-rate placement only.
- Geo-suppress alcohol commerce in ban states (Utah, Delaware) + dry counties; laws are state-by-state and actively enforced in 2025.
- FTC affiliate disclosure on every referral.

=== SEQUENCING ===
Phase 1 (0-10k MAU/metro): free claimed brewery profiles, build check-in habit, events calendar with ticketing affiliate (low-lift, clean). No revenue pressure.
Phase 2 (10-50k): turn on Verified/Featured paid placement + launch Pro tier to breweries with proven traffic.
Phase 3 (50k+): sponsored badges/challenges, then merch and e-commerce affiliate as volume garnish.

key_findings: Beer culture has a deep, ready-made vocabulary that doubles as delightful UI language: containers (pint, growler, crowler, tallboy, bomber, cottle, stovepipe, nip), serving (tap list, on tap, nitro, flight, snifter, tulip, teku, mlíko pour), metrics (ABV, IBU, attenuation), descriptors (hazy, crushable, sessionable, juicy, dank, funky, roasty, crispy), and social/collector slang (tick/ticker, whale/white whale, haul, cellar, ISO/FT, bottle share, collab). Sourced from PUNCH's modern beer terms guide and VinePair's beer geek dictionary. This is the single biggest asset for the copy layer.

Untappd is the proven gamification benchmark: check-ins (with photo, location, rating), a public earned/unearned badge collection, streaks, venue/style/country badges, and a friend feed where badge unlocks are the discovery engine ('seeing a friend earn Fruit Beer Explorer makes you want it'). Time-limited event badges (World Pint 2026) and 'Badgevent Week' drive seasonal re-engagement. Per Untappd's own updates and Wikipedia.

A 2026 arXiv longitudinal study flagged Untappd's specific ethical failures: badges that reward high-ABV (>10%) beers, quantity ('12 beers in a day'), frequency ('Drinking Your Paycheck'), early/all-day drinking ('Top of the Mornin', 'Liquid Lunch'), and consecutive-day streaks — with only token disclaimers. This is a concrete map of what NOT to gamify, and the differentiator for a responsible-but-fun app.

2026 is decisively the no/low era: the non-alcoholic beverage market is projected past $157B, Gen Z is the first sober-curious-native generation choosing by taste (60%) over price/tradition, and taprooms now treat high-quality NA beer, 'damp'/moderation lifestyles, and sober meetups as core, not fringe. An inclusive global beer app must make NA/low-ABV first-class, not a footnote.

Beer's social ritual is global and family-inclusive in 2026 (festivals with brewer access, trivia/yoga/art at taprooms, women drinkers as a named growth gap — only 3 of top-100 brands skew female). The voice must be warm and worldly, not frat-bro-exclusive, while still being playful enough for the bottle-share crowd. Lager renaissance + fruited sours + collabs are the current flavor conversation.
recommendations: Build the UI copy directly from the lexicon so features feel native to beer people: name the tasting-set feature 'Flights,' the wishlist 'The Cellar,' logging a beer a 'Pour' or 'Tick,' the saved-shop list 'Tap List,' rare finds 'Whales,' a user's beer photos a 'Haul,' and trades 'ISO / FT.' Use descriptors as filters and badges (Hazy, Crushable, Crispy, Roasty, Funky, Nitro). See DETAILS for the full lexicon table with in-app usage.

Ship a gamification system beer people actually want, centered on breadth and discovery, not volume: a Beer Passport (styles-explored map + country stamps), exploration badges (Style Explorer, Around the World, First Nitro, Cellar Curator), curated Flights as guided tasting quests, Brewery Bingo boards for taproom visits, friend feeds where badge unlocks drive discovery, and opt-in leaderboards ranked by unique styles/breweries/countries — never by quantity or ABV. See DETAILS for the full system.

Deliberately DO the opposite of Untappd's flagged mechanics: no high-ABV badges, no 'X beers in a day' quantity rewards, no all-day/early-drinking badges, no punishing consecutive-day streaks. Reframe streaks as 'Curiosity Streaks' (unique styles/breweries over time, resettable without guilt, weekly cadence not daily) so the habit rewarded is exploration, not daily consumption.

Make no/low first-class: NA and sub-3.5% beers get their own badges (Zero Hero, Low- & Slow, Damp Champion), count fully toward passport/exploration progress, and appear as a togglable lens across the whole app. This captures the $157B NA market, Gen Z, sober-curious, pregnant, driving, and observant users in one inclusive move — and is the app's clearest 2026 differentiator.

Adopt the voice/tone and responsible-drinking framing in DETAILS: playful and knowledgeable but never gatekeeping; celebrate curiosity over capacity; bake in gentle, non-preachy moderation (standard-drink awareness, a 'Know Your Pour' hydration/pacing nudge, easy 'call it a night,' and a mode that quietly favors variety over volume). Keep it fun by making responsibility feel like connoisseurship, not a lecture.
data.details:
# The Culture & Language Layer — Global Beer App (2026)

## (a) BEER LEXICON → UI COPY & FEATURE NAMES

A curated dictionary mapped to concrete in-app usage. Terms verified against PUNCH's modern beer-terms guide and VinePair's beer-geek dictionary (URLs in sources).

### Containers & serving (great for feature/label names)
| Term | Meaning | Suggested in-app usage |
|---|---|---|
| **Pint** | Standard 16oz US serving | Default pour unit; "Log a Pint"; the app's own currency/points could be "Pints" |
| **Flight** | Small curated set of tasters | Name the CURATED TASTING-SET feature "Flights" — guided quests (e.g., "IPA Journey Flight") |
| **Tap list** | The beers a venue currently has on | Name the saved/available-beers screen "Tap List"; "On Tap Near You" for discovery |
| **On tap / draft** | Served from a keg | Filter + freshness badge ("Fresh on Tap") |
| **Keg** | The vessel; also "kicked" when empty | Progress metaphor: a group challenge = "Kick the Keg" (finish a shared board) |
| **Growler / Crowler** | 32–64oz to-go glass / sealed 32oz can | "Growler List" = take-home wishlist; Crowler icon for sealed/fresh finds |
| **Nitro** | Nitrogen-infused, creamy pour | "Nitro" descriptor tag + a "First Nitro" badge |
| **Snifter / Tulip / Teku / Stange** | Aroma/style-specific glassware | "Glassware guide" per style; optional "Right Glass" tip on each beer; glassware collecting nods to real "glasswhale" culture |
| **Tallboy / bomber / nip / stovepipe** | 16oz can / 22oz / 8oz / 19.2oz | Fun format icons on logs; format filters |
| **Mlíko / side-pull** | Czech foam-forward pour | Easter-egg badge for logging one |

### Metrics & descriptors (filters, badges, tasting UI)
| Term | Meaning | Suggested in-app usage |
|---|---|---|
| **ABV** | Alcohol by volume % | Always shown; powers the responsible-drinking + NA lens (NOT a "highest ABV" badge) |
| **IBU** | Bitterness units | Shown as a "bitterness" slider on beer pages |
| **Hazy** | Cloudy NE-style IPA | Style filter + "Hazy Hunter" badge |
| **Crushable / sessionable** | Light, easy, low-ABV | "Crushable" tag; anchors the low-ABV lens; "Sessionable" filter |
| **Juicy / Dank / Funky / Roasty / Crispy** | Flavor families | Flavor-tag chips users add to a Pour; "Crispy Boi" playful lager badge |
| **Dry-hopped / wet-hopped / collab / adjunct** | Brewing techniques/ingredients | Educational tooltips; "Collab" tag links co-brewers |

### Social & collector slang (community features, playful microcopy)
| Term | Meaning | Suggested in-app usage |
|---|---|---|
| **Tick / ticker** | To mark a beer tried (verb) | Logging a beer = "Tick it" (alt to "Pour"); "You ticked 200 unique beers!" |
| **Whale / white whale** | Rare dream beer | Wishlist tier = "Whales"; "You landed a Whale!" celebration |
| **Haul** | A batch of beers acquired | User's photo gallery = "Your Haul"; "Nice Haul!" as a reaction/toast |
| **Cellar** | Where you age prized beers | Name the WISHLIST/collection "The Cellar"; "Cellar Curator" badge |
| **ISO / FT** | In Search Of / For Trade | Community trade board labels |
| **Bottle share** | Group tasting of rare beers | "Bottle Share" = create a group tasting event |
| **Untapped** | Not yet tried (play on the classic verb) | "Untapped for you" = personalized discovery row |

*Design note:* expose two register levels. Default copy is warm/plain ("Log a beer," "Beers you want to try"); a togglable "Beer-geek mode" swaps in the slang ("Tick a Pour," "The Cellar," "Whales") so newcomers aren't gatekept and enthusiasts feel seen.

---

## (b) GAMIFICATION SYSTEM BEER PEOPLE ACTUALLY WANT

Built on what makes Untappd's loop work (public badge collections + friend-feed discovery) while deliberately fixing its flagged ethical failures.

**Check-ins (the "Pour"/"Tick")** — log a beer with optional photo, rating, flavor-tag chips, glassware, and venue. The atomic action everything feeds.

**Beer Passport** — the hero feature. A stamp book of:
- **Styles-Explored Map** — a visual "wheel"/map of ~100+ styles; each unique style fills in. Progress = breadth.
- **World Map / Country Stamps** — stamp each country/region you've had a beer from ("Around the World in 80 Beers").
- **Brewery Passport** — unique breweries visited/logged.

**Badges (curiosity-weighted, never capacity-weighted)**:
- *Explorer badges*: Style Explorer, Hazy Hunter, Lager Loyalist, Sour Seeker, Around the World (10/25/50 countries).
- *Craft/knowledge badges*: First Nitro, Right-Glass Guru, Cellar Curator, Collab Collector.
- *Inclusive badges*: **Zero Hero** (NA beers), **Low-&-Slow** (sub-3.5%), **Damp Champion** (moderation streak) — these count fully toward progress.
- *Seasonal/event badges*: time-limited "Badgevent"-style drops (Oktoberfest, Dry January, local festivals) for re-engagement.
- Unearned badges shown greyed-out alongside earned ones (proven completionist driver).

**Streaks — reframed as "Curiosity Streaks"**: reward *unique* styles/breweries/countries over a **weekly** cadence, resettable with zero guilt ("Take a week off, your Cellar's still here"). Never a daily-consumption streak.

**Flights (curated tasting quests)**: editorial + brewery-partnered sets — "Intro to IPAs," "Around the Belgian Table," "NA All-Stars," "Lager Renaissance." Completing a Flight = a badge + map progress. Turns the lexicon word into the core discovery mechanic.

**Brewery Bingo**: 5×5 boards per taproom/festival/city ("try a nitro pour," "meet the brewer," "a beer over 100 IBU," "an NA option," "a collab"). Complete a line/blackout for a badge. Perfect for the family-friendly, event-driven 2026 taproom.

**Leaderboards (opt-in, breadth-based)**: rank friends by **unique styles / breweries / countries explored** — never by total volume or ABV. Weekly/all-time toggles; friends-only by default.

**Friend Feed**: shows friends' new badges, passport stamps, Hauls, and Flights completed — badge unlocks are the discovery engine. Reactions use the lexicon ("Toast," "Nice Haul!").

---

## (c) VOICE / TONE

**Personality:** the knowledgeable friend at the bar who's genuinely thrilled you're curious — never the snob quizzing you. Fun, warm, worldly, a little witty. Frat-friendly (loves a good time, playful slang, group energy) but not frat-exclusive (never crude, never "bro," never gatekeeping).

**Principles:**
- **Celebrate curiosity over capacity.** We hype trying something new, not drinking a lot. "You just unlocked a style you've never had" > "You crushed 10 tonight."
- **Global by default.** Beer is a world language — Czech pours, Belgian tables, Mexican lagers, Japanese rice ales, African sorghum beers all belong. No single country is "the" beer country.
- **Inclusive of everyone at the table.** NA/low-ABV, sober-curious, designated drivers, and pregnant users are first-class, addressed with the same enthusiasm, never pitied or lectured.
- **Playful, not preachy.** Microcopy has personality: empty state = "Your Cellar's looking thirsty." Achievement = "That's a Whale. Frame it." Error = "That pour didn't go through — let's try again."
- **Layered slang.** Default plain-language; "Beer-geek mode" unlocks the ticker/whale/cellar vocabulary for enthusiasts.

**Sample voice:**
- Onboarding: *"Beer's a big, delicious world — 100+ styles, every country with its own take. Let's fill your Passport."*
- NA lens on: *"Big flavor, zero proof. These count just as much."*
- Badge unlock: *"New stamp! You've now explored beers from 12 countries. Where next?"*

---

## (d) RESPONSIBLE-DRINKING FRAMING (that stays fun)

The differentiator: responsibility reframed as **connoisseurship**, not a lecture. A 2026 arXiv study documented exactly how Untappd's gamification normalizes risk (high-ABV/quantity/all-day/streak badges) — we invert every one of those.

- **Don't gamify the wrong things (hard rules):** no "highest ABV," no "most beers in a day," no early-/all-day-drinking badges, no daily-consumption streaks. Volume and strength are never leaderboard axes.
- **Know Your Pour:** show standard-drink equivalents on each beer (a strong beer = more than one standard drink) — framed as savvy/informed, the mark of someone who knows beer. Optional gentle daily/weekly awareness, opt-in, never shaming.
- **Pace & hydrate nudges:** subtle, occasional ("A glass of water between pours = tomorrow-you says thanks"), styled as pro-tips, not warnings.
- **"Call it a night" flow:** one tap to wrap a session, log a designated driver, or share your location with a friend. Framed as classy, not remedial.
- **No/low first-class (the inclusive core):** NA and sub-3.5% beers get their own badges, count fully toward the Passport, and have an always-available lens — capturing the $157B NA market, Gen Z, sober-curious, and everyone who wants the ritual without the alcohol.
- **Moderation as an achievement, not a penalty:** "Damp Champion," "Dry January Explorer," "Savored, Not Slammed" — moderation earns real, celebrated badges. The app makes drinking *better*, not just *more*.
